policy_statement: Build the 400 kV 'Udine–Redipuglia' power line to reduce constraints downstream of the Redipuglia junction that limit exchanges with Slovenia and affect use of local resources.
mechanism_description: Build a new high-voltage transmission line to reduce network constraints, enabling greater electricity exchanges and increased use of locally produced generation resources.
primary_causal_pathway: Grid infrastructure expansion/interconnection → ↑ system flexibility & transfer capacity → ↑ renewable integration / ↓ curtailment → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Build/upgrade transmission lines, substations, and HVDC interconnectors; implement associated grid reinforcements to reduce congestion and improve controllability.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased transfer capacity and controllable power flows reduce bottlenecks, improve balancing across regions, and allow variable renewable output to be transported to load centers; this lowers renewable curtailment and reduces reliance on peaking/thermal balancing.  
(3) Outcome: Greater renewable generation actually delivered to the grid and less fossil generation needed for energy and balancing services, leading to net reductions in electricity-sector CO₂e emissions (with possible secondary reductions from improved efficiency/reduced losses depending on project design).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling higher renewable electricity delivery (and lower curtailment) that displaces fossil-fired generation, reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Expanding transmission/HVDC reduces congestion and increases operational flexibility, which enables more renewable energy to connect and be delivered, displacing fossil generation and lowering GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “resource efficiency” could apply if the main effect were reduced losses, and “adaptation” could apply if the main effect were hazard resilience, the mechanism description centers on renewable integration via flexibility/congestion relief—i.e., direct emissions mitigation from fuel-switching in generation.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some grid projects are justified mainly for reliability, resilience, or electrification readiness; those could shift primary classification only if renewable-integration/displacement is not the dominant climate effect. Here, the stated mechanism explicitly targets flexibility and renewable integration.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism (transmission expansion/interconnection to increase flexibility and integrate renewables) has its primary climate impact through enabling higher renewable penetration and reducing fossil generation, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Note on subtypes: More granular subtype assignment (e.g., “renewable integration,” “system flexibility,” “cross-border interconnection,” “curtailment reduction”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and project specifics.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “grid expansion” policy is primarily justified/used for resilience to climate hazards (hardening, redundancy for storms/heat) or primarily for reducing technical losses without materially changing generation mix, the primary category could differ; for the stated mechanism emphasizing renewable integration, Mitigation remains the best fit.
key_indicators: "Build the 400 kV 'Udine–Redipuglia' power line"; "reduce constraints"; "limits exchanges with the Slovenian border"; "affects the use of locally produced resources"
co_benefits: Improved security of supply and grid reliability; enhanced cross-border market integration/competition
instance_edge_case_notes: “Locally produced resources” is not explicitly “renewables,” but in the context of relieving transmission congestion and enabling exchanges, the mitigation linkage via renewable integration is strong and consistent with the assigned pathway.
